title: Practical Approaches to the Genetic Diagnosis and Management of Patients With Diabetes Mellitus

items.title: Why monogenic diabetes diagnosis changes management
items.text: Accurate genetic diagnosis directs gene-specific therapy, identifies at-risk relatives, and prevents years of unnecessary insulin or inappropriate agents in misclassified type 1 or type 2 diabetes.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "accurate diagnosis through genetic testing directs treatment and management and identifies affected and at-risk family members."

items.title: How to identify patients who need monogenic diabetes testing
items.text: Refer when pancreatic autoantibodies are negative and endogenous insulin persists >3 years after diagnosis; biomarkers and the MODY probability calculator refine pretest probability in young-onset diabetes.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "These include negative pancreatic autoantibodies and continued endogenous insulin production more than 3 years after diabetes diagnosis."

items.title: Burden of misdiagnosed MODY
items.text: More than 80% of monogenic diabetes may be missed or mislabeled for years, driving unnecessary intensive insulin, adverse drug effects, and excess cost.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "more than 80% of cases go undiagnosed or are misdiagnosed for many years."

items.title: Sulfonylurea therapy for K-ATP channel neonatal diabetes
items.text: Permanent neonatal diabetes from KCNJ11 or ABCC8 variants often responds to high-dose sulfonylureas with excellent glycemic control and improved neurologic outcomes versus insulin alone.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Permanent neonatal diabetes owing to pathogenic variants in KCNJ11 or ABCC8, the genes encoding the ATP-sensitive potassium channel in the β cell, respond to high-dosage sulfonylureas"

items.title: Why GCK-MODY usually needs no treatment outside pregnancy
items.text: Glucokinase defects cause stable, regulated mild hyperglycemia from birth; discontinuing therapy does not alter A1c and long-term complication risk at this glycemic level is low.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "GCK-MODY is characterized by stable mildly elevated blood glucose due to a glucose-sensing defect."

items.title: How to manage GCK-MODY during pregnancy without amniocentesis
items.text: Fetal macrosomia risk depends on whether the fetus inherits the GCK variant; insulin is recommended only when fetal abdominal circumference rises disproportionately above the 75th percentile—amniocentesis solely for GCK genotyping is not advised.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: C) Initiation of insulin therapy should be based on second-trimester fetal growth"

items.title: Sulfonylurea sensitivity in HNF1A- and HNF4A-MODY
items.text: These MODY subtypes show marked sulfonylurea sensitivity that bypasses the primary β-cell defect, often matching or beating insulin with lower treatment burden.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "HNF1A-MODY and HNF4A-MODY exhibit a marked sensitivity to sulfonylureas, which bypass the major β-cell defect."

items.title: Extra-pancreatic features of HNF1B-MODY
items.text: HNF1B variants cause developmental renal disease, pancreatic hypoplasia, genital tract anomalies, hypomagnesemia, and hyperuricemia—accurate diagnosis enables surveillance beyond glycemia.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Heterozygous HNF1B pathogenic variants are the most common cause of developmental renal disease"

items.title: How to transition insulin-treated MODY to low-dose sulfonylurea
items.text: Confirm C-peptide/endogenous insulin; young normal-weight patients on subreplacement insulin can often stop insulin and start half the lowest glyburide tablet, titrating carefully—long duration or full replacement doses require gradual basal reduction.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: D) Stop all insulin and start glyburide 1/2 of the 1.25 mg tablet daily"

items.title: Relapsed transient neonatal diabetes and sulfonylureas
items.text: Transient neonatal diabetes from 6q24 abnormalities often relapses in adolescence or early adulthood and can be treated with low-dose sulfonylureas.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Relapse of transient neonatal diabetes due to 6q24 abnormalities can be treated with low doses of sulfonylureas."

items.title: Pitfall: variants of uncertain significance on genetic panels
items.text: A VUS must not be treated as diagnostic MODY—expert consultation is advised when report interpretation is unclear.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Interpretation of genetic testing reports can be confusing and lead to erroneous diagnosis of monogenic diabetes when a variant of uncertain significance is detected."

items.title: Why targeted genetic testing is cost-effective
items.text: Cost-effectiveness analyses support testing in appropriate young-onset cohorts, especially with biomarker pre-screening and family cascade testing.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Cost-effectiveness analyses suggest that genetic testing for monogenic diabetes in appropriate populations can be cost-saving."

items.question: A 32-year-old woman with GCK-MODY plans pregnancy. Regarding insulin therapy, what should you advise?
options: Start insulin early to prevent all macrosomia
Base insulin on second-trimester maternal glycemia only
Base insulin on second-trimester fetal growth on ultrasound
Obtain amniocentesis solely to determine fetal GCK genotype
items.explanation: Insulin is guided by fetal growth pattern reflecting inherited GCK status; invasive testing solely for GCK genotype is not recommended.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: C) Initiation of insulin therapy should be based on second-trimester fetal growth"

items.question: A 26-year-old man with genetically confirmed HNF1A-MODY has C-peptide, BMI 22.6, diabetes duration 6 years, and subreplacement insulin (glargine 8 U + 1–2 U aspart with meals). Best sulfonylurea transition plan?
options: Continue all insulin and add glyburide 0.625 mg daily
Stop meal insulin only; start glyburide 0.625 mg daily
Stop basal only; start glyburide 0.625 mg daily
Stop all insulin; start glyburide 0.625 mg daily with close glucose monitoring
items.explanation: Young, lean, short-duration MODY with documented endogenous insulin on subreplacement doses is ideal for direct transition to low-dose sulfonylurea.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: D) Stop all insulin and start glyburide 1/2 of the 1.25 mg tablet daily"

items.question: Which feature most strongly supports monogenic diabetes genetic testing?
options: Negative autoantibodies with measurable C-peptide 5 years after diagnosis
items.explanation: Negative antibodies plus persistent endogenous insulin years after diagnosis are hallmark referral criteria.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "negative pancreatic autoantibodies and continued endogenous insulin production more than 3 years after diabetes diagnosis."

items.question: Outside pregnancy, what is appropriate management of GCK-MODY?
items.explanation: Stable mild hyperglycemia rarely warrants treatment outside pregnancy; complications are uncommon at this glycemic level.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "treatment is not needed outside of pregnancy."

items.question: A patient with HNF1B-MODY is most likely to require which non-glycemic surveillance?
options: Renal imaging and monitoring of renal function
items.explanation: HNF1B causes developmental renal disease and extra-renal manifestations needing structured surveillance.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Heterozygous HNF1B pathogenic variants cause a spectrum of abnormalities that can be assessed for and managed once an accurate diagnosis is made."

items.question: Permanent neonatal diabetes due to KCNJ11 mutations is best treated with:
items.explanation: K-ATP channel mutations respond to high-dose sulfonylureas with improved glycemia and neurologic outcomes.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "respond to high-dosage sulfonylureas with excellent glycemic control and improvement in neurologic outcomes."

items.question: Approximately what fraction of young-onset diabetes is monogenic?
items.explanation: Monogenic diabetes accounts for roughly 2% of young-onset diabetes but is frequently misclassified.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "accounts for approximately 2% of young-onset diabetes."

items.question: When starting sulfonylurea in HNF1A-MODY, what dosing approach is advised?
options: Full standard type 2 diabetes dose immediately
Half of the lowest available tablet with gradual titration
Combine with DPP-4 inhibitor first line
items.explanation: Marked sensitivity warrants starting at half the lowest dose (e.g., half of 1.25 mg glyburide) and titrating.
items.reference: Case 2: "starting with one-half tablet of the lowest dose and titrating up to achieve adequate glycemic control is advised."

items.question: Why is insulin during GCK-MODY pregnancy potentially harmful?
options: Mothers may experience hypoglycemia including severe hypoglycemia
It permanently suppresses fetal insulin secretion
It is contraindicated in all pregnancies regardless of fetal genotype
items.explanation: Insulin when the fetus inherits GCK may cause maternal hypoglycemia and theoretical growth restriction in affected fetuses.
items.reference: Case 1: "Women with GCK-MODY may experience hypoglycemia, including severe hypoglycemia, when treated with insulin during pregnancy."

items.question: Transient neonatal diabetes due to 6q24 abnormalities that relapses in adolescence is often treated with:
items.explanation: Relapsed 6q24-related diabetes responds to low-dose sulfonylureas.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Relapse of transient neonatal diabetes due to 6q24 abnormalities can be treated with low doses of sulfonylureas."

items.question: Universal genetic screening of all diabetes patients for MODY is:
options: Not economically feasible; testing should be targeted
items.explanation: Testing must target high-risk phenotypes using biomarkers and clinical calculators.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "population for MODY is not economically feasible. Thus, it is important to target genetic testing to high-risk populations"

items.question: A lean young adult misdiagnosed with type 1 diabetes for 6 years but with persistent C-peptide most likely has:
options: Latent autoimmune diabetes requiring more insulin
items.explanation: Persistent endogenous insulin years after supposed type 1 diagnosis should prompt monogenic testing.
items.reference: Case 2: "Monogenic diabetes was suspected due to his ability to miss basal insulin and continued C-peptide production."

items.question: Proband diagnosis of MODY primarily helps relatives by:
options: Eliminating need for any glucose monitoring
Enabling earlier correct classification and gene-directed therapy
Proving they cannot develop type 2 diabetes
items.explanation: Cascade testing allows timely correct treatment in affected relatives.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Proband diagnosis also aids in earlier diagnosis and correct classification of diabetes in family members."

items.statement: In GCK-MODY pregnancy, insulin is always required regardless of fetal genotype.
items.explanation: Insulin is indicated only when the fetus is known or suspected not to carry the GCK variant based on growth.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Insulin therapy is recommended only when the fetus is known or suspected to not carry a GCK pathogenic variant"

items.statement: HNF1A-MODY often achieves equal or better control with sulfonylureas than with insulin.
items.explanation: Multiple studies show sulfonylurea sensitivity in HNF1A-MODY.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "A number of studies have demonstrated equal or improved control as compared with insulin therapy."

items.statement: More than 80% of monogenic diabetes cases may be undiagnosed or misdiagnosed.
items.explanation: Misclassification as type 1 or type 2 is common.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "more than 80% of cases go undiagnosed or are misdiagnosed for many years."

items.statement: Most patients with HNF1B-MODY require insulin therapy.
items.explanation: Unlike HNF1A/HNF4A, HNF1B usually needs insulin but benefits from extra-renal surveillance.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "The majority of people with HNF1B-MODY require insulin therapy."

items.statement: Amniocentesis solely to determine fetal GCK genotype is routinely recommended.
items.explanation: Invasive testing only for GCK genotype is not recommended due to pregnancy loss risk.
items.reference: Case 1: "Amniocentesis or chorionic villus sampling for the sole purpose of assessing fetal genotype for a heterozygous GCK pathogenic variant (Answer D) is not recommended"

items.statement: Discontinuing therapy in GCK-MODY does not alter hemoglobin A1c.
items.explanation: Stable regulated hyperglycemia persists without treatment effect on A1c.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "discontinuation of therapy in GCK-MODY does not alter hemoglobin A1c."

items.statement: A variant of uncertain significance on a MODY panel confirms monogenic diabetes.
items.explanation: VUS must not be overcalled; expert input is needed.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "lead to erroneous diagnosis of monogenic diabetes when a variant of uncertain significance is detected."

items.statement: High-dose sulfonylureas can improve neurologic outcomes in K-ATP neonatal diabetes.
items.explanation: Sulfonylureas treat both glycemia and neurologic features in KCNJ11/ABCC8 disease.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "respond to high-dosage sulfonylureas with excellent glycemic control and improvement in neurologic outcomes."

items.assertion: Assertion: Insulin in GCK-MODY pregnancy should be guided by fetal abdominal growth pattern.
items.reason: Reason: Fetal genotype determines whether maternal mild hyperglycemia is perceived as normal.
items.explanation: Both are true and causally linked.
items.reference: Case 1: "the risk of fetal macrosomia is based on whether the fetus inherits the heterozygous GCK pathogenic variant from the mother."

items.assertion: Assertion: A young lean HNF1A-MODY patient on subreplacement insulin can often stop insulin when starting low-dose sulfonylurea.
items.reason: Reason: HNF1A-MODY patients never produce endogenous insulin.
items.explanation: Assertion is true when C-peptide is present; the reason is false.
items.reference: Case 2: "positive C-peptide indicates that the patient continues to make insulin."

items.assertion: Assertion: Targeted genetic testing in high-risk young-onset diabetes can be cost-saving.
items.reason: Reason: Universal screening of all people with diabetes is economically feasible.
items.explanation: Assertion is supported; universal screening is not feasible—the reason is false.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Cost-effectiveness analyses suggest that genetic testing for monogenic diabetes in appropriate populations can be cost-saving."

items.assertion: Assertion: HNF1B-MODY diagnosis enables renal and extra-pancreatic surveillance.
items.reason: Reason: HNF1B variants affect only pancreatic beta cells.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; HNF1B has multisystem effects—the reason is false.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Heterozygous HNF1B pathogenic variants cause a spectrum of abnormalities that can be assessed for and managed"

items.assertion: Assertion: Relapsed transient neonatal diabetes from 6q24 abnormalities may respond to low-dose sulfonylureas.
items.reason: Reason: All neonatal diabetes requires lifelong insulin regardless of genotype.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; genotype-directed therapy contradicts the reason.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Relapse of transient neonatal diabetes due to 6q24 abnormalities can be treated with low doses of sulfonylureas."

items.assertion: Assertion: Negative pancreatic autoantibodies support considering monogenic diabetes.
items.reason: Reason: All MODY patients have positive GAD antibodies.
items.explanation: Assertion is a referral criterion; MODY is typically antibody negative—the reason is false.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "negative pancreatic autoantibodies and continued endogenous insulin production more than 3 years after diabetes diagnosis."

items.assertion: Assertion: Sulfonylureas are effective in HNF1A-MODY because they bypass the major β-cell defect.
items.reason: Reason: Sulfonylureas work only by increasing insulin sensitivity in muscle.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; sulfonylureas stimulate insulin secretion—the reason is false.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "HNF1A-MODY and HNF4A-MODY exhibit a marked sensitivity to sulfonylureas, which bypass the major β-cell defect."

items.assertion: Assertion: Long-term complications are rare at the glycemic levels seen in GCK-MODY.
items.reason: Reason: GCK-MODY causes rapidly progressive beta-cell failure requiring insulin within 2 years.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; GCK-MODY is stable—the reason is false.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "At this level of glycemic control, diabetes-related complications are rare."
